content.problemStatement:
You are given the root of a **binary tree** with NNN nodes numbered from 111 to NNN. You need to count the number of nodes in each node's **subtree**.

A **binary tree** is a tree in which each node has at most two children.

A **subtree** of a node xxx in a tree TTT is a tree consisting of the node xxx and all of its **descendants** in TTT. A **descendant** of a node xxx is any node reachable from it by repeated proceeding from a parent to its child.


Input Format


The first line of the input contains a single integer TTT - the number of test cases. The description of TTT test cases follows.




The first line of each test case contains a single integer NNN.




The second line contains NNN space-separated integers p1,p2,…,pNp_1,p_2, \ldots, p_Np1​,p2​,…,pN​ - the parents of each node. If pi<0p_i<0pi​<0 then the node iii is the **left child** of the node ∣pi∣|p_i|∣pi​∣. If pi>0p_i>0pi​>0 then the node iii is the **right child** of the node ∣pi∣|p_i|∣pi​∣.




**Note:**



For Java language, you need to:

Complete the function in the submit solution tab:


`ArrayList(Integer) countNodes(Node root){...}
`
 \  



For C++ language, you need to:

Complete the function in the submit solution tab:


`vector(int) countNodes(struct Node* root){...}
`
 \  



For Python language, you need to:

Complete the function in the submit solution tab:


`def countNodes(root):
`

Output Format

For each test case, the function you complete should return a list of NNN integers in which the iii-th (111-based indexing) number should be the number of nodes in the subtree of the node which is labeled with iii.
constraints: 1 <= |pi| <= N for all i except the root
The given structure always forms a binary tree
Sum of N over all test cases does not exceed 100000
content.inputFormat: The first line contains an integer T, the number of test cases. For each test case: the first line contains an integer N, the number of nodes. The second line contains N space‑separated integers p1 … pN, where pi = 0 for the root, pi < 0 means node i is the left child of |pi|, and pi > 0 means node i is the right child of pi.
content.outputFormat: For each test case output a single line with N integers. The i‑th integer (1‑based) is the size of the subtree rooted at node i.

sampleTestCases.explanation: First test case: node 1 is root, each node i (i>1) is the right child of i-1, so subtree sizes decrease from 5 to 1. Second test case: root is node 2. The constructed tree yields subtree sizes as shown.
Root is node 3. Nodes 1 and 2 are its left and right children respectively, each having two leaf children. Subtree sizes are computed accordingly.

solution.approach: For each test case we build two arrays left[ ] and right[ ] storing the left and right child of every node (0 if absent). The root is the node whose parent value is 0. A depth‑first search (recursive or iterative) starting from the root returns the size of the current subtree: 1 + size(left child) + size(right child). While returning we store the computed size in ans[i]. After the DFS finishes we output ans[1] … ans[N]. The algorithm visits each node exactly once, so it runs in O(N) time and O(N) memory.
solution.timeComplexity: O(N) per test case (total O(sum N))
solution.spaceComplexity: O(N) for the child arrays and answer array (plus recursion stack of depth O(N) in worst case)
